Description: Vitest is a fast Vite-powered test runner with native TypeScript support, Jest-compatible API, and built-in coverage.
Setup:
npm install -D vitest @vitest/coverage-v8
Add "test": "vitest" and "test:coverage": "vitest run --coverage" to package.json scripts. Create vitest.config.ts.
Common Mistake: Forgetting to set globals: true in vitest.config.ts and then not importing describe/it/expect in test files, leading to 'describe is not defined' errors.

Description: Jest is a widely-used test framework with built-in assertion library, mocking, and code coverage. Requires ts-jest or @swc/jest for TypeScript.
Setup:
npm install -D jest @types/jest ts-jest
Run npx ts-jest config:init to generate jest.config.ts. Add "test": "jest" to package.json scripts.
Common Mistake: Using ts-jest without proper tsconfig settings. Ensure tsconfig has "module": "commonjs" for Jest or use @swc/jest for ESM projects.

Description: Supertest allows you to test HTTP endpoints by sending real requests to your Express/Fastify app without starting a listening server.
Setup:
npm install -D supertest @types/supertest
Export your Express app instance separately from the server.listen() call so supertest can use it directly.
Common Mistake: Calling app.listen() in the same file as the app definition. This starts a real server during tests and causes port conflicts. Separate app creation from server startup.

Description: Use vi.mock to mock entire modules in Vitest. Mocks are hoisted to the top of the file automatically.
Setup: No extra setup needed. vi.mock is available globally when using Vitest. Use vi.fn() for individual function mocks.
Common Mistake: Forgetting to call vi.clearAllMocks() in beforeEach, causing mock state to leak between tests and produce flaky results.

Description: Use jest.mock to mock entire modules in Jest. Mocks are automatically hoisted to the top of the file.
Setup: No extra setup needed. jest.mock is available globally. Use jest.fn() for individual mocks and jest.spyOn for partial mocking.
Common Mistake: Using jest.mock inside a describe block or test. jest.mock calls are hoisted to the top of the file regardless of placement, which causes confusing behavior if you expect scoped mocking.

Description: Use testcontainers to spin up real Docker containers (PostgreSQL, Redis, etc.) for integration tests, ensuring tests run against real infrastructure.
Setup:
npm install -D testcontainers
Docker must be running on the CI/dev machine. Tests may take longer to start but are highly reliable.
Common Mistake: Forgetting to increase the test timeout for container startup. Containers can take 10-30 seconds to start. Set a high timeout on beforeAll.

Description: Playwright provides cross-browser end-to-end testing with auto-waiting, network interception, and built-in test runner.
Setup:
npm init playwright@latest
This scaffolds playwright.config.ts and a tests directory. Install browsers with npx playwright install.
Common Mistake: Writing flaky selectors like div:nth-child(3) > span. Use data-testid attributes or accessible roles (getByRole) for stable element selection.

Description: Snapshot tests capture the output of a function or component and compare against a stored snapshot file, making it easy to detect unexpected changes.
Setup: No extra setup for Vitest or Jest. Snapshots are stored in __snapshots__ directories automatically.
Common Mistake: Blindly updating snapshots with --update without reviewing the diff. Snapshot tests are only useful if you carefully review changes when updating.

Keywords: fixture, factory, seed, test data, builder
Description: Use fixture files or factory functions to create consistent test data. Factories generate unique but predictable data for each test.
Setup: Create a test/factories directory with builder functions for each entity. Use libraries like faker for realistic data.
Common Mistake: Hardcoding test data inline in every test. This leads to duplication, inconsistent data, and tests that are hard to maintain when the data shape changes.

Keywords: seed, database, test data, setup, teardown
Description: Seed a test database with known data before integration tests and clean up afterward to ensure test isolation.
Setup: Create seed functions that insert known data. Use transactions or TRUNCATE for cleanup between tests.
Common Mistake: Not cleaning the database between tests, causing tests to depend on execution order and creating flaky test suites.

Description: Test-Driven Development follows the Red-Green-Refactor cycle: write a failing test first, make it pass with minimal code, then refactor.
Setup: Write the test first. Run it to see it fail. Write the minimum code to pass. Refactor while keeping tests green.
Common Mistake: Writing the implementation first and then retrofitting tests. This leads to tests that confirm existing behavior rather than driving the design, and often results in untested edge cases.

Description: Configure test coverage thresholds to ensure critical code paths are tested. Use V8 or Istanbul providers in Vitest or Jest.
Setup:
npm install -D @vitest/coverage-v8
Configure coverage thresholds in vitest.config.ts or jest.config.ts.
Common Mistake: Setting coverage thresholds too high (100%) which makes minor refactors painful, or too low (50%) which provides no real safety net. Start around 80% and adjust.

Description: Properly test async functions by awaiting results, testing resolved values, and verifying rejected promises.
Setup: Use async/await in test functions. Vitest and Jest handle promises natively if the test function returns one.
Common Mistake: Forgetting to await expect().rejects.toThrow(). Without await, the test passes even if the promise does not reject, because the assertion itself returns a promise that goes unhandled.

Description: Explicitly test error cases and boundary conditions, not just the happy path. Verify correct error types, messages, and status codes.
Setup: Write dedicated test cases for each error condition. Use describe blocks to group happy path and error path tests.
Common Mistake: Only testing the happy path. If error handling code is not tested, bugs in error paths go undetected until production incidents occur.

Description: Test Express/Fastify middleware in isolation by providing mock request, response, and next function objects.
Setup: Create minimal mock objects for req, res, and next. Use supertest for full integration middleware tests.
Common Mistake: Testing middleware only through full HTTP requests. While integration tests are valuable, unit testing middleware in isolation is faster and catches logic errors more precisely.

Description: Validate that API consumers and providers agree on the request/response contract using schema validation or tools like Pact.
Setup:
npm install -D @anatine/zod-openapi
Define your API schemas with Zod and validate actual responses against them in tests.
Common Mistake: Only checking status codes without validating response body shape. A 200 response with a wrong body structure is still a broken contract.

Description: React Testing Library encourages testing components the way users interact with them, using queries based on accessible roles and text content.
Setup:
npm install -D @testing-library/react @testing-library/jest-dom @testing-library/user-event
Configure your test environment to use jsdom.
Common Mistake: Testing implementation details like component state or internal method calls. Test what the user sees and interacts with, not internal component structure.

Description: Mock process.env in tests to control configuration without relying on actual environment variables.
Setup: Use vi.stubEnv in Vitest or manual assignment with cleanup in Jest.
Common Mistake: Modifying process.env directly without restoring original values in afterEach, causing environment variable leaks between tests and flaky results.

Keywords: fishery, factory, test data, builder, pattern
Description: Use the fishery library to create type-safe test data factories with support for traits, sequences, and transient parameters.
Setup:
npm install -D fishery
Create a factories directory and define a factory for each domain entity.
Common Mistake: Creating test data with plain object literals in every test. This leads to massive duplication and makes refactoring entity shapes extremely painful.

Description: Use it.each or test.each to run the same test with different inputs, reducing duplication for data-driven tests.
Setup: Use it.each with a table of inputs and expected outputs. Add descriptive test names using template literals.
Common Mistake: Writing separate test functions for each input when the test logic is identical. This creates unnecessary code duplication and makes it harder to add new test cases.

Description: Test Node.js EventEmitter-based code by listening for events and asserting they fire with correct data.
Setup: Use once() with promises for async event testing. Use vi.fn() to create spy listeners.
Common Mistake: Not handling the case where an event never fires. Use Promise.race with a timeout or AbortSignal to avoid tests hanging indefinitely on missing events.

Description: Test WebSocket servers by creating real client connections in tests and asserting on messages and connection lifecycle.
Setup:
npm install -D ws @types/ws
Start the WebSocket server in beforeAll and close it in afterAll. Create client connections per test.
Common Mistake: Not closing WebSocket connections in tests, leading to handle leaks and tests that hang or fail intermittently on CI.

Description: Use autocannon to load-test HTTP endpoints and measure throughput, latency, and error rates.
Setup:
npm install -D autocannon @types/autocannon
Start your server before running benchmarks. Use autocannon programmatically in test files.
Common Mistake: Running performance tests in CI alongside unit tests. Performance tests are slow and results vary by machine. Run them separately with dedicated infrastructure.

Description: Test Prisma queries against a real database to ensure your data access layer works correctly with actual SQL.
Setup: Use a test database with Prisma migrations applied. Clean tables between tests using $executeRawUnsafe or transactions.
Common Mistake: Mocking PrismaClient entirely in integration tests. Mocking the ORM defeats the purpose of testing data access logic. Use a real test database instead.

Description: Test Zod schemas to verify they accept valid data and reject invalid data with correct error messages.
Setup: No extra setup needed. Import your Zod schemas and use safeParse to test both valid and invalid inputs.
Common Mistake: Using schema.parse() in tests instead of schema.safeParse(). parse() throws on invalid input, making it harder to assert on specific validation error details.
